facts.fact: PostgreSQL 12+ automatically inlines non-recursive, side-effect-free, singly-referenced CTEs by default, removing the optimization fence; multi-referenced CTEs or those marked MATERIALIZED remain fenced
In PostgreSQL 17, materialized CTEs with specific sort order allow the planner to reuse that information in outer queries, improving performance by avoiding redundant sorting

DuckDB's USING KEY recursive CTE variant treats intermediate results as keyed dictionaries instead of sets, enabling row updates rather than accumulation, reducing memory and runtime significantly for graph algorithms
PostgreSQL 12 introduced NOT MATERIALIZED keyword allowing explicit control over CTE materialization; MATERIALIZED keyword forces materialization even when CTE is referenced once
Databricks SQL enforces two safety limits on recursive CTEs: maximum recursion depth of 100 steps and row limit of 1 million, failing with error if either threshold exceeded
PostgreSQL full-text search using GIN index with tsvector achieved ~877ms on 10-million-row dataset, representing 50× speedup over unoptimized baseline
For 200 million row dataset, PostgreSQL full-text search queries with composite filters (time + FTS + severity) completed in under 1 second when properly indexed with GIN
PostgreSQL tsvector setweight function assigns weights A(1.0), B(0.4), C(0.2), D(0.1) to document regions, allowing ts_rank() to weight title matches higher than body matches during relevance ranking

Recursive CTE performance can be optimized using DuckDB's USING KEY which prevents redundant path accumulation; in distance-vector routing, USING KEY generates substantially fewer rows than vanilla WITH RECURSIVE
PostgreSQL has no built-in incremental materialized view refresh; pg_ivm extension provides immediate trigger-based updates supporting inner joins and DISTINCT but not outer joins or window functions

JSONB performance in PostgreSQL varies non-linearly with data size: 10K rows show 10× speedup over normalized, but at 500K rows performance can collapse due to TOAST overhead at 2kB document threshold
PostgreSQL functional (expression) indexes are expensive to maintain on writes but provide fast indexed lookups; execution time improvement example showed 0.086ms with functional index vs baseline
Neo4j performs 180× faster than MySQL for 3-hop friend-of-friend-of-friend queries; performance advantage grows exponentially with relationship depth while MySQL performance degrades 5-9×
CTEs can cause cardinality underestimation: column statistics from CTE scan results aren't propagated upward, causing outer query planner to miscalculate selectivity
Recursive CTE depth limits: PostgreSQL allows arbitrary depth; DuckDB limits to implementation; best practice is to add explicit termination condition checking if node already visited
Databricks SQL replacing legacy code with recursive CTEs achieved 12× speedup: hierarchical data preparation reduced from ~6 minutes to ~30 seconds
PostgreSQL REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW acquires ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock, blocking all reads/writes until completion; concurrent refresh with CONCURRENTLY requires unique index but allows concurrent reads
Set-based WHILE loops avoiding Halloween protection often outperform traditional recursive CTEs when traversing hierarchies 8-13 levels deep
High-cardinality column statistics in PostgreSQL can be 30× smaller than actual values on very large tables (hundreds of millions rows) due to ANALYZE sample size being too small
Row explosion in joins: aggregating first before join can reduce 5 million rows down to 2 rows, dramatically improving performance; detect in EXPLAIN via loops > 1 on subquery nodes in DML

analogies.analogy: CTEs historically act like a sealed box: the outer query planner can't see inside to push filters down. PostgreSQL 12+ punches a hole in the box for single-use CTEs (inlining), but MATERIALIZED re-seals it.

Vanilla recursive CTEs are like photocopying each step of a path-finding search: every iteration adds to a growing pile of papers (union table), even papers that describe outdated paths. DuckDB USING KEY treats it like updating entries in a dictionary instead.

REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW is like stopping traffic at a busy intersection to repave it (ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock). REFRESH CONCURRENTLY is like keeping one lane open for traffic while you work, but requires a unique marker (index) on each lane.

The planner's statistics are a loan officer; CTEs are like handing off the application to a different loan officer who doesn't have the credit history. They have to guess—and usually guess conservatively, leading to wrong decisions.

analogies.concept: JSONB vs normalized tables scale cliff
analogies.analogy: JSONB is like keeping everything in a single drawer: fast when the drawer is small, but once it exceeds PostgreSQL's 2kB TOAST threshold, the database splits it across multiple drawers (TOAST chunks), and performance falls off a cliff.
Weighting A=1.0, B=0.4, C=0.2, D=0.1 is like a grading rubric: title matches earn A's (full points), keywords earn B's (40%), body matches earn C's (20%), and metadata earn D's (10%).

analogies.concept: Neo4j vs SQL for deep relationship queries
analogies.analogy: SQL is like finding directions by asking 10 people sequentially (each join is a person you ask); Neo4j is like having a map where you can see all paths at once. The gap grows exponentially with relationship depth.

common_misconceptions.misconception: All CTEs are optimization fences that prevent predicate pushdown
common_misconceptions.reality: Only true before PostgreSQL 12. Since v12, single-use CTEs are automatically inlined and optimize normally. Multi-use CTEs remain fenced unless marked NOT MATERIALIZED (which requires careful evaluation).

common_misconceptions.misconception: JSONB is always faster than normalized tables in PostgreSQL
common_misconceptions.reality: JSONB wins at small scale (< 100K rows) and for whole-object reads. At 500K+ rows or frequent partial updates, performance collapses due to TOAST overhead (2kB threshold) and full-document rewrite cost of jsonb_set().

common_misconceptions.misconception: Recursive CTEs are suitable for all graph traversal use cases
common_misconceptions.reality: Vanilla recursive CTEs accumulate obsolete rows in the union table. For shortest-path or distance-vector routing, DuckDB USING KEY or specialized graph databases are needed. SQL is inefficient for multi-hop deep traversal.

common_misconceptions.misconception: PostgreSQL materialized views can be incrementally updated like typical database views
common_misconceptions.reality: PostgreSQL has no built-in incremental refresh; REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW rebuilds the entire view and acquires ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock. pg_ivm extension provides incremental updates but lacks support for outer joins and window functions.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Full-text search in PostgreSQL is slow compared to Elasticsearch
common_misconceptions.reality: Properly indexed PostgreSQL FTS (GIN index + tsvector, fastupdate=off) achieves sub-second queries on 200M rows. It's a viable alternative for small-to-medium applications and avoids operational overhead of a separate search cluster.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Query planner cardinality estimates are accurate for large tables with many columns
common_misconceptions.reality: ANALYZE sample size is fixed (~30K rows default); on tables with hundreds of millions of rows and high-cardinality columns, n_distinct estimates can be 30× too small, causing planner to choose wrong join strategy.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Functional (expression) indexes are always beneficial for fast queries
common_misconceptions.reality: Functional indexes are expensive to maintain on writes (index must be recomputed for every INSERT/UPDATE). Use only when query speed is more important than write speed. Not suitable for write-heavy workloads.

common_misconceptions.misconception: MATERIALIZED keyword in PostgreSQL has the same semantics as in other databases
common_misconceptions.reality: PostgreSQL's MATERIALIZED is an optimization hint (force inlining or force materialization) that varies by version. Before v12, all CTEs were materialized. In v12+, semantics changed to allow fine-grained control.

common_misconceptions.misconception: SQL databases are suitable for deep relationship queries (e.g., social networks)
common_misconceptions.reality: SQL requires N joins for N-hop queries. Neo4j is 180× faster for 3-hop queries and the gap widens exponentially. Use specialized graph databases for relationship-heavy workloads, not normalized SQL.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Slowly changing dimensions Type 2 is optimal for all historical tracking
common_misconceptions.reality: SCD Type 2 rows balloon quickly if data changes frequently. Partitioning by effective date range and choosing the right SCD type based on change frequency and query patterns is critical for performance.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Tsvector ranking is a simple relevance score from 0–1
common_misconceptions.reality: ts_rank() returns arbitrary numeric values based on document length and query term frequency. Weights A–D are multipliers (1.0, 0.4, 0.2, 0.1) applied per region. Normalize or calibrate empirically for 0–1 output.
